g.g:
Stacks: Introduction, Stack Operations, Stack Implementation Definition of a Stack
<text x="0" y="70" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="A stack is a linear data structure that follows the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle. It is called a stack because it is similar to a physical stack of plates. Plates are added and removed from the top of the stack.

<text x="0" y="20" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="Properties of a Stack
<text x="0" y="40" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="Elements in a stack are added and removed from the top using two operations: push and pop. The last element inserted into the stack is the first one to be removed.

<text x="0" y="20" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="Types of Stacks
<text x="0" y="40" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="Array Stack: A stack implemented using an array. Link Stack: A stack implemented using linked lists.

<text x="0" y="20" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="Push Operation
<text x="0" y="40" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="The push operation adds an element to the top of the stack. Example: stack.push(5) adds 5 to the top of the stack.

<text x="0" y="20" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="Pop Operation
<text x="0" y="40" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="The pop operation removes the top element from the stack. Example: stack.pop() removes the top element from the stack.

<text x="0" y="20" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="Peek Operation
<text x="0" y="40" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="The peek operation returns the top element of the stack without removing it. Example: stack.peek() returns the top element of the stack without removing it.

<text x="0" y="20" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="isEmpty Operation
<text x="0" y="40" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="The isEmpty operation checks if the stack is empty. Example: stack.isEmpty() returns true if the stack is empty, false otherwise.

<text x="0" y="20" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="Array Stack Implementation
<text x="0" y="40" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="An array stack is implemented using an array. The push operation adds an element to the top of the array, and the pop operation removes the top element from the array.

<text x="0" y="20" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="Link Stack Implementation
<text x="0" y="40" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="A link stack is implemented using linked lists. The push operation adds an element to the top of the linked list, and the pop operation removes the top element from the linked list.

<text x="0" y="30" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="Push Operation
<text x="0" y="50" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="The push operation adds an element to the top of the stack. Example: stack.push(5) adds 5 to the top of the stack.

<text x="0" y="30" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="Pop Operation
<text x="0" y="50" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="The pop operation removes the top element from the stack. Example: stack.pop() removes the top element from the stack.

<text x="0" y="30" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="Peek Operation
<text x="0" y="50" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="The peek operation returns the top element of the stack without removing it. Example: stack.peek() returns the top element of the stack without removing it.

<text x="0" y="30" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="isEmpty Operation
<text x="0" y="50" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="The isEmpty operation checks if the stack is empty. Example: stack.isEmpty() returns true if the stack is empty, false otherwise.

<text x="0" y="30" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="Stacks are used in a variety of applications, including parsing expressions, implementing recursive algorithms, and evaluating postfix notation.

<text x="0" y="30" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="Stacks are used to parse expressions by scanning the input string and pushing and popping tokens onto the stack until the end of the string is reached.

<text x="0" y="30" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="Stacks are used to implement recursive algorithms by pushing and popping values onto the stack as needed.

<text x="0" y="30" text-anchor="start" font-size="14" font-family="Arial, sans-serif" fill="#1a1d23" data-narration="Stacks are used to evaluate postfix notation by pushing and popping values onto the stack as needed.

g.text: Stacks: Introduction, Stack Operations, Stack Implementation
